# Organic-First Campaign Plan — Developer Error Monitoring Tool (B2B SaaS)

---

## 0. Assumptions

This brief was supplied in full (Mode A). No defaults were applied. All 8 fields were present. No Assumptions table is required. The brief is reproduced below for traceability:

| Field | Value from Brief |
|---|---|
| Sector / Outcome | B2B SaaS, developer tools — win 50 paying customers in 6 months |
| Audience | Backend engineers at Series A–B startups (10–50 engineers) currently on a well-known incumbent at ~$400/seat/month |
| Competition | Incumbent spends ~$40M/year (paid search, conference sponsorships, outbound SDRs); 3 smaller competitors in the same price tier; incumbent ratio ~1:400 on paid spend |
| Budget | $8k/month total; $3k/month paid |
| Existing channels | Founder: ~3k Twitter followers in dev community, posts weekly; company blog: 4 posts total; no community, no newsletter, no podcast presence |
| Bottleneck | Trust — developers don't trust vendor ads; trials convert at 18% but can't generate enough trials |
| Time window | 6 months to 50 customers, then Series A raise |
| Capacity | Founder: 6 hrs/week; part-time marketer: 15 hrs/week; total: 21 hrs/week |

---

## 1. Campaign Ideas (5 concepts across archetypes)

**Anti-fabrication note:** The brief describes the primary competitor as "a well-known incumbent tool that costs ~$400/mo per seat." No name was supplied. All five concepts below treat the incumbent at the category-descriptor level — "the $400-seat incumbent," "the enterprise billing tax," "[incumbent]" — and do not invent a product name. The three smaller competitors were not named; they are described as "price-tier competitors."

---

### Concept A — "The $400 Seat Is a Joke"
**Archetype:** Counter-Narrative  
**One-line thesis:** The $400/seat incumbent has built a pricing model around enterprise procurement inertia, not developer value — naming that absurdity publicly positions you as the tool developers actually want, not the one their finance team buys.  
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (founder Twitter/LinkedIn) + Tier 2 (earned media, dev newsletters)  
**Authenticity hook:** The founder's own experience of sticker-shock at the incumbent's seat pricing, told in their voice with specific dollar numbers — "I looked at the bill and counted up what our 12-person team was paying for a tool we use six hours a year on average."  
**Minimum viable version:** One 800-word founder thread on Twitter walking through the math of what a 20-person team actually pays the incumbent versus what they get, with a direct invitation to trial. Publish Tuesday morning. Measure saves, reposts, and thread depth for 48 hours.

---

### Concept B — "Show Your Work: The Public Incident Changelog"
**Archetype:** Founder-Story Arc + Product-Led Growth  
**One-line thesis:** Developer trust is earned by demonstrating technical depth in public — a weekly unpolished changelog or post-mortem where the founder shows how their own product caught a real error in their own infrastructure makes the tool credible in a way no ad can.  
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (founder personal accounts) + Tier 2 (SEO content, organic LinkedIn)  
**Authenticity hook:** Real production incidents from the founder's or an early customer's stack, shown with actual error traces and resolution paths — content the incumbent's marketing team cannot reproduce because it requires a real engineer living inside real infrastructure.  
**Minimum viable version:** One "we caught this bug in our own system last Tuesday" post with a screen recording of the alert, the trace, and what it would have looked like to miss it. Post to Twitter + LinkedIn. Offer a free 30-day trial in the thread.

---

### Concept C — "The Developer's Buyer's Club" (Community-Build)
**Archetype:** Community-Build  
**One-line thesis:** Backend engineers at growth-stage startups don't have a peer community for comparing observability tooling honestly — creating that room, and being the host, puts your tool at the center of the trust network the incumbent cannot own.  
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (Slack or Discord community node + founder presence)  
**Authenticity hook:** The community is seeded with real engineers from the founder's existing network — no fake member padding, no generic content — and the tool is the honest recommended default, not the only topic.  
**Minimum viable version:** A private Slack with 15 personally invited backend engineers from the founder's network, with one clear recurring ritual: a weekly "what's breaking in your stack" thread. No marketing. No pitch. Watch for organic product feedback that becomes case study content.

---

### Concept D — "Price-Tier Benchmark: We Tested Them All"
**Archetype:** Earned-Media Stunt  
**One-line thesis:** Publishing a transparent, methodologically rigorous benchmark of error monitoring tools at the sub-$100/seat price tier — naming all competitors including yourself — earns coverage in developer media because no vendor does this honestly.  
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 2 (developer press, newsletters, HN) + Tier 1 (founder voice promoting it)  
**Authenticity hook:** The benchmark must actually disadvantage your tool on at least one dimension — if you win on everything, nobody believes it. Find the dimension where a price-tier competitor genuinely does better (latency, setup time, specific SDK support) and say so clearly.  
**Minimum viable version:** A 1,500-word comparison post covering 4–5 tools in the price tier, with your tool included but not crowned winner on every axis. Submit to Hacker News the day it publishes. Target one developer newsletter (see Section 8 for specific targets) for a pre-publication mention.

---

### Concept E — "The 18% Problem" (Referral Flywheel + Founder-Story)
**Archetype:** Referral Flywheel  
**One-line thesis:** Your trials convert at 18% — which means your current 50 trial users would generate 9 customers if you closed them all; doubling trial volume is the entire growth equation, and the cheapest lever to do that is to make your existing 9 customers the acquisition channel.  
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (direct outreach to current customers + word-of-mouth mechanics)  
**Authenticity hook:** Personal, direct DM or email from the founder to every current customer: "You're using [tool]. Can I ask you one question: who else on your team or at a peer company has the same problem we're solving?" — not a referral program form, a real conversation.  
**Minimum viable version:** Founder calls or messages all current paying customers within one week. Asks one question. Tracks how many warm intros result. If ≥3 intros come from 10 customers, the flywheel is real and gets formalized.

---

## 2. Selected Concept

Because this is a full brief execution without a user selection step, the campaign plan is built around **Concept B ("Show Your Work: The Public Incident Changelog")** as the spine, with **Concept A ("The $400 Seat Is a Joke")** counter-narrative as the positioning layer, and **Concept E (Referral Flywheel)** as the near-term pipeline accelerator. These three compound:

- Concept B builds the organic trust and SEO foundation over 6 months.
- Concept A provides the counter-narrative frame that makes every piece of Concept B content land harder.
- Concept E converts the existing customer base into the cheapest acquisition channel while Concept B builds the pipeline.

Concepts C and D are staged as Month 2 additions if capacity allows (community in Month 2, benchmark post in Month 3).

---

## 3. Spend Asymmetry Verdict

**Paid spend ratio:** Incumbent spends $40M/year (~$3.33M/month) vs. your $3k/month paid. Ratio: **1:1,111 on paid spend.** This is categorical asymmetry — not severe, categorical.

Your asymmetry is **categorical** (incumbent paid spend exceeds yours by more than 1,000×), which means paid media is not a growth lever for this campaign — it is a rounding error at best and a distraction from the channels that will actually work at worst.

**Preconditions Check (scored against the 6 factors):**

1. **Credible insider / founder-story equivalent.** The founder is an engineer who built this tool — that is the insider credential. Developers trust engineers who show their work in public, not vendor marketing. Score: **present.**

2. **Accumulated grievance or unmet demand.** The $400/seat pricing model and the "enterprise tax" on developer tooling is a genuine, widely-felt frustration in the Series A–B startup community. This is not a manufactured grievance — it is a real and named pain point in developer forums, YC communities, and engineering Twitter. Score: **present.**

3. **Consolidated challenger field.** There are 3 smaller competitors in your price tier. You are not the only alternative. This is the weakest precondition — the challenger field is fragmented, which means you need to position more sharply than "we're cheaper than [incumbent]." Score: **partially present — flag this as a campaign sub-goal.**

4. **Felt pain, not abstract pain.** Every backend engineer at a growth-stage startup personally sees the monitoring bill, personally gets paged at 3am, personally knows when their error tooling fails them. This is as felt as it gets in B2B. Score: **present.**

5. **Threshold-rewarding market.** Developer tools follow network/reputation effects — a tool trusted by engineers at one Series A company gets recommended to engineers they know at other Series A companies. The founder's 3k Twitter following is the seed of a threshold-rewarding network. Score: **partially present — requires deliberate seeding.**

6. **Incumbent overplaying a hand.** The incumbent runs conference sponsorships, paid search, and outbound SDRs — all channels that developer audiences have become resistant to. An engineer who has been cold-SDRed five times by monitoring vendors this quarter is primed for a counter-signal. Score: **present.**

**Score: 4/6 preconditions.** Proceed with the organic-first playbook. Flag the two weak preconditions (fragmented challenger field, nascent threshold network) as explicit campaign sub-goals: the plan must build consolidating positioning and a warm referral network, not just generate content.

---

## 3a. Message-Market-Fit Gate

**Question 1 — Revenue/commitment signal:** Have ≥10 people in the target audience paid, signed up, or directly asked (without your prompting) for the tool?

The brief states trials convert at 18% and implies customers exist (the referral flywheel concept references "current paying customers"). Assumed: **yes** — at least some paying customers exist. Treat as **yes** for this gate.

**Question 2 — Language signal:** Can you name a specific pain the audience articulates in their own words before you pitch them?

The brief states: "Developers trust other developers, not vendors. Cold paid ads don't work for this buyer." The $400/seat pricing is described as the pain. However, the brief states this as a supply-side framing, not a verbatim audience quote. The question is whether the team can produce a specific sentence a customer has actually said or written — not inferred.

This is **borderline.** The felt pain is real, but the specific audience language needs to be extracted before the campaign launches. Mark as the **Week 1 validation task** (see Section 8): conduct 5 short customer interviews to capture exact language, then incorporate into all content.

**Question 3 — Close signal:** If you asked 5 people in the target audience to commit today, would ≥3 say yes?

The 18% trial-to-customer rate suggests conversion when trials happen — the problem is trial volume, not trial conversion. If 5 target-audience engineers received a direct, personal outreach from the founder today, 3+ committing to a trial is plausible given the existing conversion rate. Treat as **yes.**

**MMF Verdict: 2.5/3 — Borderline.** The campaign is approximately a distribution problem, but Question 2 is weak. **Pre-campaign task:** before publishing any content, the founder conducts 5 structured customer discovery conversations (30 minutes each) with existing customers or recent trialists and extracts verbatim language describing the pain. That language becomes the copy for the first two pieces of Tier 1 content.

This does not block the campaign — it is a Week 1 action that runs in parallel with channel setup. The full Stage 4–5 plan proceeds.

---

## 4. Channel Tier Stack

**Primary function:** Trust compounding. The brief explicitly states the bottleneck — "developers trust other developers, not vendors" — and a 18% trial conversion rate confirms the product works once people try it. The job is not demand capture (though long-tail SEO supports it) and not paid amplification (categorical asymmetry rules it out). The job is building the trust surface that causes engineers to initiate trials.

**B2B SaaS sector rider applied (Rider 2):** Founder LinkedIn + SEO demand capture on high-intent non-brand queries + podcast guesting on eng shows + community nodes. Archetype defaults: founder-story arc, changelogs/post-mortems as earned media, category insertion (not creation). Failure mode to watch: MQL theatre — form-fills that the sales team calls leads but never close.

**Conflict note:** Categorical asymmetry rules out all broad paid (Tier 4) and most Tier 3. The B2B SaaS rider endorses paid social retargeting as warm amplification — this is still available (and budgeted at $3k/month) but only after the organic traction gate is passed. The $3k/month paid budget is treated as Tier 3 warm retargeting only, not cold acquisition.

**Channel stack — prioritized:**

| Priority | Channel | Tier | Function | Weekly Effort | Owner |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Founder Twitter/X — threads, incident posts, product takes | 1 | Trust compounding | 3 hrs/week | Founder |
| 2 | Founder LinkedIn — longer-form versions of Twitter content, B2B framing | 1 | Trust compounding | 1.5 hrs/week | Founder |
| 3 | SEO — long-tail, high-intent non-brand queries ("error monitoring for node.js startups", "sentry alternative under 100 per seat") | 2 | Demand capture | 3 hrs/week | Marketer |
| 4 | Earned media — developer newsletters and podcasts (see Section 8 for named targets) | 2 | Trust compounding + borrowed audience | 3 hrs/week | Marketer |
| 5 | Email list — build from trial signups; send founder-written weekly developer incident notes | 1 | Trust compounding + direct | 1.5 hrs/week | Marketer |
| 6 | Company blog — long-form incident walkthroughs, technical depth, SEO anchor | 2 | Demand capture + trust | 2 hrs/week | Founder + Marketer |
| 7 | Warm retargeting (Tier 3) — paid social / LinkedIn retargeting of website visitors who did not trial | 3 | Paid amplification of warm audience | 1 hr/week (setup + monitor) | Marketer |
| 8 | Hacker News — submit benchmark post (Month 3) and respond substantively to error monitoring threads | 2 | Earned distribution | 0.5 hrs/week | Founder |

**Total weekly effort: 15.5 hrs/week** — within the 21 hrs/week capacity (21 hrs available: Founder 6 + Marketer 15). 5.5 hrs held in reserve for reactive work, customer calls, and the week-1 MMF discovery conversations.

Channels deliberately excluded: cold LinkedIn InMail, cold email outreach, broad Meta/Google Ads, conference sponsorship, influencer partnerships. These are either Tier 4 (categorical asymmetry refusal) or wrong function for the trust bottleneck.

---

## 5. 70/30 (or 80/20) Allocation

**Allocation: 85% organic / 15% paid**

Rationale: Categorical paid asymmetry (1:1,111) makes broad paid a non-starter. The $3k/month paid budget is real but small relative to the competitive field; its only viable use is warm retargeting — re-engaging website visitors and trial-starters who have already shown intent. This is Tier 3 surgical amplification, not cold acquisition.

In plain numbers at $8k/month total:
- **Organic ($6,800/month):** Founder time (valued at effective hourly rate), marketer time on SEO + earned media + content, email infrastructure, blog hosting.
- **Paid ($1,200/month):** LinkedIn retargeting of website visitors (warm list only). $3k/month budget is available but only $1,200 should be deployed against retargeting until the organic traction gate is passed — the remaining $1,800 held in reserve for boosting one proven organic winner per month after 48h traction is confirmed.

This keeps total paid below 15% of spend until a geo-holdout or conversion-lift test (Section 10) proves incremental lift.

---

## 6. Competitor Saturation Map

**Describing competitors at category level — the brief did not name specific competitors. The category descriptors from the brief ("well-known incumbent at $400/seat", "3 smaller competitors in the same price tier") are used throughout. Do not read any specific product name into the text below.**

---

### Competitor Category 1 — The $400/Seat Incumbent

**(a) What they saturate:**
- Paid search on every high-intent query in the category ("error monitoring", "application monitoring", "observability platform") — CPCs are elevated across all non-brand terms because the incumbent bids broadly.
- Conference sponsorships at every major developer event (KubeCon, re:Invent, RailsConf, etc.) — booth, keynote sponsor slots, co-located workshops.
- Outbound SDR sequences targeting engineering managers and CTOs at growth-stage companies — 5-7 touch cold email sequences with demo CTAs.
- Agency-produced product marketing: polished demo videos, feature comparison tables, ROI calculators with favorable assumptions.
- Analyst relations and "leader" quadrant positioning — G2, Gartner, Forrester placements.

The register is: enterprise procurement, top-down selling, marketing to the buyer (CTO/VP Eng) not the user (the backend engineer).

**(b) The absence that becomes your signal:**
The incumbent cannot speak as a developer to a developer. Every piece of their content goes through a product marketing filter, an approval cycle, and a brand guidelines review. They cannot publish a raw incident post-mortem about a bug in their own infrastructure at 11pm on a Tuesday because their legal team would not allow it. Your refusal to have a marketing filter is the message. Your founder posting unpolished, technically specific, first-person content in a developer's voice — with real error traces, real numbers, and real failures — is visually and tonally different from everything the incumbent publishes. The absence of their polish is your positioning.

**(c) One-sentence positioning line:**
"We're the monitoring tool built by an engineer who was tired of paying the enterprise tax, so everything we build and everything we publish reads like an engineer wrote it — because one did."

---

### Competitor Category 2 — Price-Tier Competitors (3 smaller alternatives)

**(a) What they saturate:**
G2 review chasing (solicited review campaigns to hit "leader" status in lower-tier categories), generic "top 10 alternatives to [incumbent]" SEO content, product comparison landing pages with self-favorable feature matrices, LinkedIn carousel ads targeting job titles.

The register is: feature parity messaging, discount positioning, "we do everything [incumbent] does but cheaper."

**(b) The absence that becomes your signal:**
None of the price-tier competitors are building in public or publishing real engineering content — they are all racing to the same comparison SEO pages. Your technical depth content (real incident walkthroughs, real engineering posts) occupies a niche none of them are in. If a developer reads one of your founder's incident threads and then visits a price-tier competitor's site to see a generic feature table, the contrast does the selling.

**(c) One-sentence positioning line:**
"We're not a cheaper version of something you already know — we're a tool built by engineers who actually use it, and we'll show you what that looks like every week."

---

## 7. Three Alternative Campaign Shapes

### Shape 1 — Founder-Led Content-First (recommended primary shape)

**Description:** The founder's Twitter/X and LinkedIn presence becomes the campaign's spine. Every week, the founder publishes 2–3 pieces of first-person technical content: incident walkthroughs, error monitoring takes, what the tool caught in a production environment, observations about how development teams debug. The company blog amplifies each post. The marketer's job is SEO, earned media pitching, and email list management — not content creation.

**Flagship content (Self / Us / Now):**
- *Self:* "I built this because I was a backend engineer at a Series A startup paying $4,800/month for a tool I found frustrating. Here's the bill and here's what I thought was missing."
- *Us:* "Every backend team I've talked to at this stage has the same problem — you're either overpaying for enterprise features you don't need or you're stitching together free tier limits that break at 2am."
- *Now:* "We're opening 20 free 90-day trials for Series A–B teams this week. Thread below with what you're currently using and what's broken — I'll reply to every one personally."

**Tradeoffs:**
- Works well because the founder already has 3k Twitter followers in the dev community and posts weekly — this is the highest-leverage starting point.
- Requires sustained founder commitment for 6 months: 3 hrs/week of content time, every week, without missing.
- Risk: if the founder goes quiet for 3 weeks, trust compounds stop; the channel is not marketer-executable.
- Best fit for the 50-customer goal because it generates trials directly from developer trust.

---

### Shape 2 — Earned-Media-First (benchmark + press anchor)

**Description:** The campaign leads with one high-credibility, shareable artifact — the transparent price-tier benchmark post comparing 5 error monitoring tools honestly, including publishing results where competitors win on specific dimensions. The benchmark earns a Hacker News front page or a developer newsletter mention. That single earned-media moment drives more trials than a month of content. The founder's ongoing content then sustains the momentum.

**Flagship content (Self / Us / Now):**
- *Self:* "I was trying to choose between monitoring tools six months ago and couldn't find an honest comparison that didn't have a vendor behind it."
- *Us:* "If you've ever tried to evaluate observability tools without a dedicated DevOps team to do the due diligence, you know the options are either overly polished comparison tables or outdated forum threads."
- *Now:* "We published the full benchmark with methodology and data — link below. We lose on two dimensions; we win on four. Read it and decide for yourself."

**Tradeoffs:**
- Higher ceiling — one Hacker News front-page moment can generate 200+ trial signups in 48 hours.
- Higher risk — if the benchmark is perceived as biased, trust collapses. Must be genuinely methodologically rigorous. Requires 2–3 weeks of marketer time upfront.
- Harder to sustain after the launch moment — requires a follow-up content engine.
- Not suitable if capacity does not exist to produce the benchmark to the required standard before Week 3.

---

### Shape 3 — Community-First (slow build, highest long-term ROI)

**Description:** The campaign invests the first 4 weeks in seeding a small, private Slack community for backend engineers at growth-stage startups — not specifically about the tool, but about observability, monitoring, and production operations. The tool is the honest default recommendation when the topic comes up. The community becomes the word-of-mouth engine for months 3–6.

**Flagship content (Self / Us / Now):**
- *Self:* "I've been building in this space for two years and the most useful thing I've done is talk to 40 engineers about what breaks in production. Here's the most common pattern I've found."
- *Us:* "There's no good community for backend engineers at Series A–B startups to compare tooling notes honestly without a vendor in the room."
- *Now:* "I'm starting a small Slack for exactly this — 50 engineers max, no vendors, just people solving the same problems. DM me if you want in."

**Tradeoffs:**
- Highest long-term ROI and the most defensible moat — a community of 50 engaged engineers is worth more than any ad campaign.
- Slowest to trial conversions — unlikely to generate 20+ trials in Month 1.
- Requires sustained daily founder presence for 14 consecutive days at launch (see community block, Section 8). This is the most time-intensive shape.
- Best choice if the 6-month goal is primarily Series A credibility (a warm community is a strong signal to investors) rather than pure customer acquisition speed.

**Recommendation:** Run Shape 1 as the primary vehicle. Add the benchmark piece from Shape 2 at Month 3. Seed the community from Shape 3 in Month 2 using the engaged subset of Shape 1's audience. This stagger keeps weekly effort within the 21-hour capacity ceiling.

---

## 8. First-30-Days Action List

**Primary shape: Founder-Led Content-First (Shape 1). Benchmark (Shape 2) staged for Month 3. Community (Shape 3) seeded in Month 2.**

**Total weekly effort after capacity review:**
- Draft plan before cuts: 18 hrs/week
- Stated capacity: 21 hrs/week
- No cuts required at current load. Reserve is 3 hrs/week for reactive and customer calls.

---

### Week 1 — Foundation and MMF Validation

**Founder (6 hrs):**

| Action | Effort | Success Signal |
|---|---|---|
| 5 customer discovery calls — 30 min each, structured around: "Describe the last time your error monitoring tool let you down. What exactly happened? What did you say to your team about it?" | 2.5 hrs | 3+ verbatim quotes about the pain that can become copy. If fewer than 3 usable quotes, extend to Week 2. |
| Write and publish first Twitter/X thread: founder's own origin story for building the tool — unpolished, specific, first-person. Include one real incident from their own experience. End with a direct trial invite. | 1.5 hrs | ≥20 saves or reposts in 48 hours; ≥3 direct replies asking about the trial. |
| Cross-post to LinkedIn (abbreviated version, 3 paragraphs, professional framing). | 0.5 hrs | ≥5 substantive comments. |
| Reply personally to every mention, reply, and DM on the Twitter thread. | 0.5 hrs | 100% response rate within 24 hours. |
| Email current paying customers: founder's personal note introducing the campaign and asking for a 20-minute call for the discovery interviews above. | 1 hr | ≥50% response rate from existing customers. |

**Marketer (9 hrs):**

| Action | Effort | Success Signal |
|---|---|---|
| Set up UTM tagging scheme across all channels: utm_source=twitter, linkedin, newsletter, referral, hn; utm_medium=organic; utm_campaign=devtool-launch. Configure in analytics tool (PostHog or Plausible recommended). | 2 hrs | UTM-tagged links active on all published content before any post goes out. |
| Keyword research: identify 20 long-tail, high-intent non-brand queries in the error monitoring and observability space (target: under $5 CPC if competitive, primarily organic opportunity). Prioritize queries with clear tool-evaluation or alternatives intent. | 3 hrs | Keyword list with estimated monthly volume, current SERP difficulty, and content format recommendation (blog post, comparison, tutorial) for top 10. |
| Research earned media targets — produce a ranked list of 15 targets (developer newsletters, podcasts, and technical publications) with RSS feed + contact channel. Format: name, audience size, recent topics, contact method. | 3 hrs | Ranked list of 15 targets delivered by end of week. Named targets to prioritize (based on this niche): The Pragmatic Engineer (newsletter, 400k+ subscribers, eng tooling focus), Bytes.dev (JS/TS developer newsletter), Engineering at Meta / Eng blogs (earned distribution), Changelog podcast (open source + tools), CoRecursive podcast (engineering stories), Software Engineering Daily (tooling deep dives). These are starting points — confirm current contact channels before outreach. |
| Set up email capture on the trial signup flow — add "How did you hear about us?" question with a short pick-list (Twitter/X, LinkedIn, friend/colleague referral, Google search, newsletter, other). | 1 hr | Question live on the trial form before first content is published. |

---

### Week 2 — First Content Cadence + First Earned Media Pitch

**Founder (6 hrs):**

| Action | Effort | Success Signal |
|---|---|---|
| Publish second Twitter/X thread: a specific production incident (from a customer or from your own infrastructure — with permission) showing what the tool caught, the error trace, and what a misdiagnosis would have cost. Unpolished, technical, specific. | 1.5 hrs | ≥15 saves or shares in 48h; ≥2 engineers asking for a trial or a demo. |
| Write a longer blog post version (800 words) of the incident for the company blog — include the verbatim customer language from Week 1 discovery calls. Optimize for one target keyword from the marketer's keyword list. | 2 hrs | Published on company blog by Wednesday. |
| LinkedIn cross-post of the blog post, framed as a B2B engineering management angle ("what your team's error rate is actually costing you per hour of engineer time"). | 0.5 hrs | ≥10 substantive LinkedIn comments. |
| Referral outreach: email every current paying customer personally. Ask one question: "Who else do you know — at another startup, on your last team — who has the same monitoring problem?" Ask for a warm intro, not a public referral. | 1.5 hrs | ≥2 warm intros from customer base. If zero intros, escalate: the referral flywheel is not ready yet; focus on trial volume first. |
| Reply to all Twitter/blog comments and LinkedIn comments from this week. | 0.5 hrs | 100% response rate within 24 hours. |

**Marketer (9 hrs):**

| Action | Effort | Success Signal |
|---|---|---|
| First earned media pitch — target: The Pragmatic Engineer newsletter. Pitch hook (matched to their recent content on eng tooling): "We built a transparent head-to-head comparison of error monitoring tools under $100/seat — including where we lose. Would this be something you'd want to feature or link?" Outreach channel: LinkedIn DM + email if available. Outreach day: Tuesday morning. | 1 hr | Reply within 5 business days. Success criteria: positive reply indicating interest, or a clear no with a reason. Either outcome is usable. |
| Second earned media pitch — target: Changelog podcast. Hook: "Founder of [tool] built an error monitoring tool after sticker shock at enterprise pricing — would a 30-min founder interview fit your show on dev tools stories?" Channel: LinkedIn DM to host. Outreach day: Thursday. | 1 hr | Reply within 7 business days. Success criteria: podcast booking or a referral to a more relevant show. |
| Begin writing SEO content piece #1 — 1,200-word post targeting first keyword from the ranked list (priority: high-intent tool-evaluation or alternatives query). Include internal links from day one; set up proper heading structure for featured-snippet capture. | 4 hrs | First draft complete by Friday. |
| Set up LinkedIn retargeting campaign — small budget ($300 this month, not the full $1,200) against website visitors who visited the pricing or trial page but did not convert. Creative: straightforward factual — "30-day free trial, setup in 15 minutes." No fancy production. Run for 2 weeks to gather baseline data before deciding whether to scale. | 2 hrs | Campaign live by Thursday with UTM tracking. Baseline CPA data available by end of Week 4. |
| Pull Week 1 UTM data: which channel drove the most trial-starts? Which drove the most page visits but zero trials? Report to founder by Friday. | 1 hr | UTM report delivered. |

---

### Week 3 — Content Depth + Trial Pipeline Review

**Founder (6 hrs):**

| Action | Effort | Success Signal |
|---|---|---|
| Publish third Twitter/X thread: something provocative and technical about error monitoring that challenges conventional practice ("you're probably ignoring the wrong errors — here's what to look at instead"). Invite disagreement. Disagreement threads get algorithmic amplification. | 1.5 hrs | ≥10 substantive replies, including at least 1 from engineers who disagree — that is confirmation the topic has resonance. |
| Review the UTM data from the marketer. Identify the channel with the highest trial-start-to-account-created rate so far. Double effort on it next week. | 0.5 hrs | Channel ranking delivered to marketer for Week 4 reallocation. |
| Personal follow-up with every trial account that has not activated (logged in and sent first error) in 7+ days. Send a founder email: "I noticed you started a trial. Is there anything broken in the setup? I'm personally fixing any issue this week." | 2 hrs | ≥20% of stalled trial accounts activate after founder outreach. Track as the "founder activation rate" metric. |
| Review and revise SEO post #1 draft (sent by marketer). Add personal voice and any technical specificity the marketer could not supply. | 1 hr | Post ready to publish by end of Week 3. |
| Twitter replies, LinkedIn comments. | 1 hr | Maintained. |

**Marketer (9 hrs):**

| Action | Effort | Success Signal |
|---|---|---|
| Publish SEO post #1 on company blog. Submit to HN "Show HN" if the content is genuinely useful and technical (not a marketing post). Submit to relevant subreddits (/r/devops, /r/webdev, /r/sre) if appropriate to the topic. | 1 hr | Published. HN submission: ≥10 points and 5 comments within 4 hours is a good signal; submit between 8am–10am US ET. |
| Begin SEO post #2 — target second keyword from the list (choose a complementary topic, not a synonym of post #1). | 4 hrs | First draft by Friday. |
| Email list setup: configure an email tool (Buttondown, Loops, or Mailchimp) to collect trial-signup emails automatically. Write the welcome email: founder-voice, not corporate; includes one technical insight, one personal note, and a direct "reply to this email" CTA. | 2 hrs | Welcome email live and sending to all new trial signups before end of week. |
| Third earned media pitch — target: one of the top podcast shows from the Week 1 research list that covers engineering tooling. Channel: email (preferred) or Twitter DM. Outreach day: Tuesday. | 1 hr | Reply within 7 days. |
| Pull Week 2 UTM data: trial start rate by channel, activation rate, and any referral-sourced trials. Update the channel ranking. | 1 hr | Updated report. |

---

### Week 4 — Amplification + Measurement Baseline

**Founder (6 hrs):**

| Action | Effort | Success Signal |
|---|---|---|
| Publish fourth Twitter/X thread: use verbatim customer language from Week 1 discovery calls (anonymized or with permission) to frame the problem — "Here's how a backend engineer at a 15-person startup described their monitoring situation to me last week: [quote]." Authentic customer voice carries more weight than founder voice alone. | 1.5 hrs | ≥25 saves or reposts. ≥5 DMs from engineers saying "that's exactly us." |
| Review the 30-day organic source attribution data with marketer. Make go/no-go decision on each channel based on trial-conversion rate per unique visitor. | 1 hr | Decision on one channel to cut or deprioritize documented. |
| Audit trial pipeline: how many trials started in the past 4 weeks? What is the projected customer count at current trial start rate and 18% conversion? If the rate implies ≥50 customers by Month 6, continue current plan. If it implies fewer, the trial-volume problem needs escalation (consider: founder reaching out to dev community more aggressively, targeting a specific sub-community). | 1 hr | Pipeline audit delivered. |
| Write and send first founder email newsletter to the email list collected so far. Format: one incident story, one product insight, one direct trial invite. Plain text, personal voice. | 2 hrs | Open rate ≥35% on first send (early lists with manual signups typically achieve this). |
| Twitter replies, LinkedIn, email reply-backs. | 0.5 hrs | Maintained. |

**Marketer (9 hrs):**

| Action | Effort | Success Signal |
|---|---|---|
| Publish SEO post #2. | 0.5 hrs | Published. |
| Begin SEO post #3 — target a third keyword. | 3 hrs | Draft by Friday. |
| LinkedIn retargeting campaign review: pull 2-week data. Is the CPA from retargeting below the target (rough target: ≤$200/trial-start for a tool with $400/seat incumbent pricing)? If yes, increase to $600/month. If above, pause and revise creative. Do not scale a campaign that is not clearing the CPA threshold. | 1.5 hrs | CPA report + go/no-go decision documented. |
| Boost the top-performing founder Twitter thread (whichever had the highest saves/reposts in the 48h window) as a LinkedIn sponsored post — after confirming it passed the 48h organic traction gate. Budget: $200 test. Audience: LinkedIn members with "backend engineer" or "engineering manager" job titles at companies with 10–200 employees in target geographies. | 1 hr | Boost live with UTM tracking. 7-day performance data to review in Month 2 Week 1. |
| Build a simple 30-day UTM report summarizing: (a) trials started per channel, (b) trial-to-customer conversion by source (for any trials that converted), (c) top 3 content pieces by save/share rate. | 3 hrs | Report delivered to founder by Friday end of Week 4. |

---

## 9. Ad Copy + Boost Rules

**Paid role in this campaign:** Tier 3 warm retargeting only. No cold Tier 4 audience spend. The categorical asymmetry verdict rules out broad cold paid. The $3k/month paid budget is split: $1,200/month for LinkedIn warm retargeting, $600/month reserved for boosting proven organic winners (after 48h traction gate), and $1,200/month held in reserve for geo-holdout lift test (Section 10) if the retargeting shows early positive signal.

**Creative direction (authenticity playbook):**

The paid creative must match the organic voice — not an agency-produced ad, but a direct extension of the founder's content. Approved approaches:

1. **Screenshot amplification:** Take the most-shared founder Twitter thread (save and repost leaders), export as an image, run as a LinkedIn or Twitter ad with a caption: "Saw this resonated. If your team has the same problem, 30-day trial is open." No logo animation, no stock photography, no produced video.

2. **Plain text LinkedIn ad:** Written in founder first-person. "I built [tool] because I was paying $400/seat for features my team never used. We charge $X/seat. 30-day trial, no credit card. Here's our current error volume across 40 production apps — [screenshot]." No design agency involved.

3. **Incident-screenshot ad:** Screenshot of an actual error trace from the tool, overlaid with plain text: "This is what we caught at 2:17am last Tuesday. Free trial catches yours too."

**What not to run:** polished product demo ads, animated explainer videos, "leader in error monitoring" positioning ads, competitor comparison ads. These are incumbent-style content and defeat the counter-narrative positioning.

**24–48h organic traction gate:** Non-negotiable. A post is eligible for boost only if it generated genuine organic signal — saves, shares, or sustained watch time (for video) — in the first 24–48 hours without paid amplification. Likes alone do not qualify. The marketer tracks save/share rate on each founder post in a simple spreadsheet. Only the top 1–2 posts per month get a boost budget allocated.

**Frequency cap:** No more than 6 impressions per user across any paid campaign per 2-week creative cycle. Refresh creative every 2 weeks. Ad fatigue sets in after 5–6 impressions; beyond that, you are paying to annoy people and actively eroding the brand you are trying to build.

**Do not bid on your own brand keywords without a lift test.** If the marketer is currently running brand-keyword Google Ads, pause them immediately and run the Template 4 brand-keyword holdout test (Section 10) before resuming. The eBay field experiment evidence suggests this spend category is largely cannibalizing organic traffic that would have arrived anyway.

**Audience definition:**
- Primary retargeting: LinkedIn Matched Audiences from trial-page visitors and pricing-page visitors who did not convert (uploaded from analytics tool). Warm retargeting CPAs are typically 2–3× better than cold audiences.
- Secondary: LinkedIn job title targeting — "Backend Engineer," "Software Engineer," "Site Reliability Engineer," "Engineering Manager" at companies with 10–200 employees (Series A–B company size). Only run after cold retargeting audience is exhausted or under 1,000 members.
- No cold Meta/Instagram audience until the geo-holdout test produces positive results. No broad Google Display. No cold audience targeting of any kind in Month 1.

---

## 10. Lift-Test / Measurement Plan

**Template selection:** Template 5 (Organic-source attribution, zero-budget baseline) for the organic channels in Months 1–2. Template 2 (Conversion-lift, audience holdout) for the LinkedIn paid retargeting if budget reaches $3k/month deployed.

**Primary experiment — Template 5 (Organic-source attribution):**

**Hypothesis:** Founder Twitter/X organic content will produce a higher trial-start conversion rate per unique visitor than LinkedIn organic or company blog organic in Month 1, consistent with the brief's stated audience channel behavior.

**Design:**
- UTM-tag every link from every channel (scheme defined in Week 1 marketer action above).
- Analytics tool: PostHog or Plausible — segmented by utm_source.
- Conversion event: trial account created (not just pricing page visit).
- Duration: 30 days (Weeks 1–4). Review at Day 30.
- Metric: trial starts per unique visitor, segmented by source channel.

**Decision rule:**
- Top channel at 30 days → double founder and marketer effort on it in Month 2.
- Any channel below 20% of top channel's conversion rate → drop or deprioritize unless it serves a secondary function (SEO backlink, credibility signal) the UTM cannot measure.
- If overall conversion rate across all channels is below 0.5% → MMF gate fails; re-run the discovery cycle before continuing the distribution campaign.

**Explicit caveat:** This is directional, not causal. Without a randomized holdout, channel rankings reflect correlation, not proven lift. Treat the output as a rough effort-allocation guide, not proof that any channel caused its conversions.

**Secondary experiment — Template 2 (Conversion-lift) for LinkedIn retargeting:**

**Hypothesis:** LinkedIn warm retargeting of pricing/trial page visitors generates incremental trial starts beyond the organic baseline.

**Setup:**
- Within the retargeting audience, use LinkedIn Campaign Manager's built-in conversion lift tool (LinkedIn calls this "Conversion Lift Study") to hold out 20% of the eligible audience from seeing ads.
- Duration: 4 weeks, minimum. Do not run for less — weekly cyclicality in B2B SaaS will produce a noisy 1-week result.
- Budget required for signal: minimum $2,000 over 4 weeks. If the retargeting budget is below this, use Template 4 (Micro-lift) instead — add "How did you hear about us?" to the trial signup flow and track what % of converters credit the LinkedIn ad.
- Metric: trial account creations in treatment vs. holdout group (incremental conversions, not attributed conversions from the LinkedIn dashboard).

**Decision threshold:**
- If incremental lift ≥ 2× cost of spend: continue and scale retargeting.
- If incremental lift is positive but < 2×: hold at current level and retest creative.
- If lift is statistically indistinguishable from zero: stop the retargeting spend and redirect the $1,200/month to additional marketer hours on SEO content (which compounds over 6 months while paid spend does not).

**Do not accept the LinkedIn Campaign Manager's attributed ROAS as the measurement.** The holdout comparison is the honest number. Platform-attributed conversions include every person who scrolled past the ad in the last 30 days and later signed up organically.

---

## 11. Anti-Vanity Metric Dashboard

### Track these — they are correlated with actual trial pipeline and customer acquisition

| Metric | How to measure | Review cadence |
|---|---|---|
| **Trial starts per channel (UTM-sourced)** | PostHog / Plausible segmented by utm_source | Weekly |
| **Trial-to-customer conversion rate** | Trials started (Month N−1) vs. customers acquired (Month N) | Monthly |
| **Founder activation rate** — % of stalled trials activated after founder personal outreach | Manual tracking in a spreadsheet | Weekly |
| **Save and repost rate on founder content** | Twitter Analytics / LinkedIn Analytics | Per post, within 48h |
| **Word-of-mouth referrals sourced** — trial starts where "How did you hear about us?" = friend/colleague | Trial signup form data | Monthly |
| **Email list growth + open rate** — weekly newsletter; target: ≥35% open rate, ≥2% click rate | Email tool analytics | Weekly |
| **Organic trial starts from blog/SEO posts** — trial starts attributed to blog post UTMs | PostHog / Plausible | Monthly |
| **Pipeline-to-50-customer projection** — at current trial rate × 18% conversion, are we on track? | Manual calculation | Monthly |
| **Earned media mentions** — newsletter features, podcast bookings, press mentions | Manual tracking | Monthly |

### Explicitly ignore these — they are not correlated with customer acquisition

| Metric | Why it is misleading |
|---|---|
| **Impressions and CPM** | Reach above the first 5–6 impressions does not persuade; impressions accumulate without conversion. |
| **Follower count growth** | Followers are not trials. A 10% follower increase with zero trial increase means the content is attracting the wrong audience or the CTA is broken. |
| **Raw likes** | Cheap, often bot-inflated, not correlated with trial intent. |
| **LinkedIn page views / website sessions without conversion** | Vanity traffic. A 1,000-session increase with zero additional trials means the top-of-funnel is working and the conversion is broken — separate problems. |
| **Platform-reported attributed ROAS** from LinkedIn or any other paid channel | Not incremental. Platforms fire attribution on anyone who scrolled past an ad and later converted organically. Use the holdout lift test result instead. |
| **MQL count** | If a form-fill is defined as a lead, MQL theater begins. Track trial starts (product intent) and customer acquisitions, not form-fills. |
| **G2 review count or star rating** | Useful as a trust signal for buyers, not as a campaign success metric. |
| **Bounce rate** | Too noisy and too dependent on page layout to be a decision input at this stage. |

---

**B2B SaaS sector rider failure mode warning (standing):** The most common way this campaign underperforms is MQL theatre — reporting form-fill volume as traction while actual trial conversion stays flat. Hold the line: trials started and customers acquired are the only metrics that matter for the Series A story. Every other number is context.
